data.title: Merit Medical outlines 2026 constant-currency revenue growth of 5.6% to 7% while integrating $140M View Point Medical deal
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Merit Medical Systems (MMSI) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We reported total revenue of $381.9 million, up 7% year-over-year on a GAAP basis and up 5% year-over-year on a constant currency basis," said (President, CEO & Director Martha Aronson), adding that "our non-GAAP operating margin increased 47 basis points year-over-year to 19.7%, representing the highest first quarter operating margin in the company's history" and that the company generated "$25 million of free cash flow."

  • "On April 1, building upon our oncology platform, we announced the acquisition of View Point Medical for an aggregate transaction consideration of $140 million, of which $90 million was paid in cash at closing," said (CEO Aronson), describing OneMark as "an expansion of the annual addressable procedure opportunity of approximately 3x for our oncology business" and adding that "the combination of SCOUT and OneMark provides physicians with localization options during the initial diagnostic biopsy."

  • "We are now reporting our revenue in 2 product categories: foundational and therapeutic," said (CEO Aronson), stating that the company plans to report revenue results by those categories while continuing to provide "additional color on the underlying drivers of growth" in the eight internal platforms.

  • "First quarter net income was $56.7 million or $0.94 per share compared to $52.9 million or $0.86 per share in the prior year period," said (CFO & Treasurer Raul Parra), and added that "first quarter net income and EPS exceeded the high end of our guidance range by $3.7 million and $0.07, respectively."

Outlook

  • "For the 12 months ending December 31, 2026, we now expect total GAAP net revenue growth in the range of 6.3% to 7.8% year-over-year and 5.6% to 7% year-over-year on a constant currency basis," said (CFO Parra), adding that guidance assumes "sales of foundational products increase in the mid-single digits" and "sales of therapeutic products increase in the high single digits" year-over-year.

  • "We continue to expect non-GAAP diluted earnings per share in the range of $4.01 to $4.15," said (CFO Parra), while noting, "our non-GAAP EPS range reflects the $0.05 of dilution from the acquisition of View Point Medical, funded by the better-than-expected non-GAAP EPS results we delivered in the first quarter."

  • "We expect our total revenue in the range of $400 million to $410 million" for Q2 2026 and "non-GAAP EPS in the range of $0.90 to $1," said (CFO Parra), while guiding Q2 non-GAAP operating margins of "approximately 18.7% to 20.4% compared to 21.2% last year."

  • Compared with the Q4 2025 call, the company updated 2026 guidance to include View Point Medical, raising expected 2026 inorganic revenue contributions to "approximately $17 million to $20 million compared to $13 million to $15 million previously," per (CFO Parra).

Financial results

  • Q1 revenue was $381.9 million; (CFO Parra) said the quarter included U.S. sales up $14.5 million (6.8%) and international sales up $4.1 million (3%).

  • (CFO Parra) reported non-GAAP gross margin of 53.2% and said Q1 gross margin included "a $4.6 million impact from tariffs... representing a 120 basis point impact" versus none in the prior-year period.

  • (CFO Parra) reported Q1 non-GAAP operating income of $75.3 million and an operating margin of 19.7%; he also reported free cash flow of $24.7 million and said cash and cash equivalents were $488.1 million at March 31, 2026, with total debt obligations of $747.5 million and adjusted net leverage of 1.6x.

Q&A

  • Michael Petusky, Barrington Research: asked for WRAPSODY update; (President, CEO & Director Aronson) said, "we've given... our revised guidance in 2026 of $7 million for WRAPSODY for the fiscal year, and we're tracking right on that."

  • Michael Petusky, Barrington Research: asked about tariff refunds; (CFO Parra) said, "We have started the process of reimbursement" and added, "if things stay as they are today, I definitely think that the $15 million would come down."

  • Jason Bednar, Piper Sandler: asked about View Point ramp and synergies with SCOUT; (CEO Aronson) said adding OneMark expands the market "3x to 4x" and described the opportunity as "a physician choice about whether they'd rather use Radar technology or ultrasound technology"; (CFO Parra) added, "at the midpoint of our '27 guide... you can definitely tack on the 20% that we called out."

  • Jason Bednar, Piper Sandler: pressed on OEM recovery and strategic value; (CFO Parra) said, "We are selling our own product. Divesting of that just doesn't really work" and added that for Q2 he was "starting to see orders" and "we should be at the very least at that mid-single digits growth profile."

  • Sam Eiber, BTIG: asked about prior-quarter supply dynamics and geopolitics; (CFO Parra) said a supply issue "did turn into a recall" and added, "from a financial perspective, it's immaterial to our 2026 financial results"; on the Middle East, he said the company left "about $1.5 million of revenue on the table" but called it "very manageable."

  • Michael Matson, Needham: asked about share repurchase; (CFO Parra) said, "for now... we're just focused on CGI" and cited "a lot of opportunity out there from an M&A perspective."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone skewed slightly negative on discrete risks and visibility (tariff refunds, OEM volatility, geopolitical disruption, recall), with pointed questions such as whether OEM’s "worst is behind you" and how quickly View Point ramps.

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and execution-focused in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, using confidence language such as (CEO Aronson) "we remain confident" and (CFO Parra) "we feel like we can overcome whatever is coming our way," while acknowledging uncertainty that tariffs are "a moving target."

  • Versus Q4 2025, Q1 carried more operational risk discussion (tariff reimbursement timing, Middle East shipping disruption, recall clarification), while management continued emphasizing the CGI framework.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 introduced a completed deal and updated reporting, with (CEO Aronson) highlighting the View Point Medical acquisition and the shift to reporting "foundational and therapeutic" revenue, after Q4 framed the intent to streamline external reporting.

  • Guidance language stayed anchored to the same 2026 non-GAAP EPS range of $4.01 to $4.15 and maintained the assumed tariff burden of $15 million, while Q1 increased expected 2026 inorganic revenue contributions to $17 million to $20 million from $13 million to $15 million.

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s heavy emphasis on gross margin expansion and WRAPSODY launch setup to Q1’s scrutiny of OEM order timing, tariff refunds, View Point ramp economics, and geopolitical/logistics impacts.

Risks and concerns

  • (CFO Parra) flagged OEM volatility, noting OEM sales declined 14% year-over-year in Q1 and that U.S. orders were "lower than expected" due to "inventory destocking dynamics," which he characterized as "transient or timing based rather than a reflection of share loss."

  • Tariff uncertainty remained a key variable: (CFO Parra) said 2026 guidance assumes a "12-month tariff impact of approximately $15 million or $0.19 per share" and added the situation "continues to be an evolving situation," while the company began seeking reimbursements.

  • Operational disruption risk included logistics constraints in the Middle East (including "about $1.5 million" of delayed revenue) and a recall discussed as "immaterial" to 2026 results, per (CFO Parra).

Final takeaway

Merit Medical management emphasized a Q1 start that included 5% constant-currency revenue growth, 19.7% non-GAAP operating margin, and $0.94 EPS, while integrating the $140 million View Point Medical acquisition and changing external revenue reporting to foundational and therapeutic categories. For 2026, management reiterated non-GAAP EPS of $4.01 to $4.15, projected constant-currency revenue growth of 5.6% to 7%, and framed tariffs, OEM timing, and geopolitical shipping disruption as the key swing factors it plans to reassess with the Q2 update.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Meta layoffs: Zuckerberg blames higher AI spending, won't rule out further cuts
data.description:
Meta (META) CEO Mark Zuckerberg said the company's planned ​job cuts were due to increased capital spending for artificial intelligence and did not rule out further layoffs, Reuters reported.

"We basically have two major cost centers in the company: compute infrastructure and people-oriented things," he said during a town hall on Thursday. "If we're ​investing more in one area to serve our community, then that means we ​have less capital to allocate to the other. So that means we ⁠do need to take down the size of the company somewhat."

But Zuckerberg clarified that the layoffs were not related to Meta's (META) reorganization of teams around a new AI-native structure and efforts to develop AI agents that can perform tasks autonomously.

Meta's (META) AI-oriented reorganization and a new initiative to track employees' mouse movements, clicks ​and keystrokes to train AI agents have faced criticism from staff.

"Getting everyone internally to use AI tools and getting to do the work more efficiently is not the thing that's driving layoffs," Zuckerberg told employees.

But "we'll see how all this stuff trends," he said, adding that the company ​would "be able to ​share more soon."

The town hall was the first time Zuckerberg addressed staff directly about the job cuts. Meta (META) is set to lay off about 10% of its workforce next month, with additional ⁠cuts planned for the second half of the year.

"I wish that I can tell you ⁠that I ​have a crystal ball plan for the next, like, ​three years of how all this stuff is going to play out. I don't. I don't think anyone does," ​the CEO told employees at the town hall.

data.title: Ingram Micro forecasts Q2 net sales of $13.6B-$14.0B while expanding buyback and raising dividend 2.4%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ingram Micro Holding Corporation (INGM) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We delivered another strong first quarter in which we grew net revenue nearly 14%... and delivered non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.75.” (CEO & Director Paul Bay)
  • “Advanced Solutions and Cloud led to growth, driven in part by large GPU and AI infrastructure deals we captured in North America and Asia Pacific in the back half of the quarter.” (CEO & Director Bay)
  • “Xvantage is not a tool or a marketplace. It is the operating system for B2B.” (CEO & Director Bay)
  • “In the first quarter alone, it processed approximately 230,000 e-mails into orders, up 78% year-over-year, enabling more than $1 billion in sales with significantly lower manual touch.” (CEO & Director Bay)
  • “Net sales of $13.96 billion were up 13.7% year-over-year... and up 10% on an FX-neutral basis.” (Executive VP & CFO Michael Zilis)
  • “Gross margin came at 6.63% of net sales, down 12 basis points year-over-year.” (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)

Outlook

  • “We are guiding net sales of $13.6 billion to $14.0 billion.” (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)
  • “We expect non-GAAP diluted EPS to be in the range of $0.68 to $0.78 per diluted share.” (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)
  • “While we are not necessarily projecting outsized GPU and AI infrastructure projects in our guide, we will continue to participate in these projects.” (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)
  • “Included in this guide is a potential negative impact of $0.01 to $0.03 per diluted share... from the volatile situation in the Middle East.” (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)
  • Versus the prior quarter’s outlook framing, management’s Q2 guide explicitly baked in Middle East risk and reiterated that memory supply constraints were assumed to have “a similar impact in Q2” as in Q1. (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)

Financial Results

  • “Gross profit came in at $926 million, up 12% year-over-year.” (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)
  • “Q1 operating expenses were $703 million or 5.04% of net sales compared to 5.11% in the same period last year.” (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)
  • “Adjusted income from operations was $262 million, up 14% year-over-year.” (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)
  • “Non-GAAP net income in the quarter was $175.5 million compared to $144.2 million... an increase of 22%.” (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)
  • “Adjusted free cash flow was an outflow of $962 million.” (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)
  • “We announced we are further expanding the repurchase program for future use... [and] announced an increase in the next quarterly dividend of 2.4% sequentially and 10.5% over the prior year.” (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)

Q&A

  • Katherine Murphy, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.: Asked which projects/products are seeing deferrals or scope changes due to costs; Executive VP & CFO Zilis said it is “more project-based... more on the Advanced Solutions area” and “more geared towards smaller customers,” while CEO & Director Bay cited “one instance in a smaller country in Europe” with a PC configuration constrained, now “a quarter or two out.”
  • Katherine Murphy, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.: Asked about back-half IT demand; CEO & Director Bay said “we only guide 1 quarter at a time,” adding expectations that CES will “grow at market” and Advanced Solutions and Cloud “above market,” while noting SMB remains “more near term” and the company is seeing “resiliency in the business.”
  • Matt Dezort, William Blair: Asked about lead times/order dynamics and budget shifts; Executive VP & CFO Zilis described “reallocation... a shift in scope,” plus shipping delays tied to the Middle East and OEM allocation to higher-demand AI products.
  • Matt Dezort, William Blair: Asked for Phase 3 Xvantage progress and margin delta; CEO & Director Bay pointed to AI models “getting better,” said IDA engagements were “up 50% year-over-year,” and reiterated an end-of-year target for IDA revenue share in Xvantage countries to be “double digits.”
  • Maya Neuman, Morgan Stanley: Asked about risk to cost-plus pricing and margin pressure; Executive VP & CFO Zilis said price increases are “a pass-through,” and added, “we're not necessarily going to be conceding margin,” while CEO & Director Bay cited substitutes/bundles and said the company is “starting to see some movement” from on-prem to cloud.
  • David Paige Papadogonas, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about Q2 by-region expectations and CES components; Executive VP & CFO Zilis indicated continued strength in APAC, noted potential EMEA overhang from Middle East risk, and said within CES, PCs remain supported by refresh while mobility has a “harder compare.”
  • Adam Tindle, Raymond James: Asked about AI infrastructure capabilities and whether INGM would be ODM-like; CEO & Director Bay said, “that is not in our plans today,” emphasizing facilitation of GPUs/AI infrastructure across servers/networking/storage, and highlighted the “Enable AI program.”
  • Adam Tindle, Raymond James: Pressed on operating leverage vs. mix; Executive VP & CFO Zilis pointed to SG&A leverage and said, “if it were not for the GPU and AI infrastructure projects, we actually have a year-over-year uptick of nearly 20 basis points in our gross margin.”
  • Ruplu Bhattacharya, BofA Securities: Asked about endpoint unit assumptions and SMB vs. enterprise; CEO & Director Bay cited PC growth “in... the mid- to high teens for the quarter,” while Executive VP & CFO Zilis said CES is tempered by smartphones and reiterated Cloud strength in “Infrastructure as a Service.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, centering on inflation/memory constraints, deferrals, lead times, mix and operating leverage (e.g., Adam Tindle questioned why EBIT was “growing in line with revenue”).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and solution-oriented, repeatedly emphasizing resilience and execution (e.g., CEO & Director Bay: “I am confident that Ingram Micro will continue executing... regardless of the uncertainties.”).
  • Versus the prior quarter, the dialogue shifted from tariffs/pull-forward monitoring to more direct discussion of memory-driven inflation, deferred projects, and an explicit EPS risk range tied to the Middle East.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 emphasized AI-enabled automation outcomes and IP milestones, expanding from Q4’s focus on two patents to Q1’s update that “4 of our 35-plus patent-pending applications have been granted.” (CEO & Director Paul Bay)
  • Mix pressure from GPU/AI infrastructure was more prominent in Q1, with management quantifying a larger margin mix effect than the prior quarter discussed, while reiterating low cost-to-serve and working-capital efficiency. (Executive VP & CFO Michael Zilis)
  • Capital return messaging evolved from Q4’s authorization and dividend cadence to Q1’s secondary offering update, a $75 million repurchase from the majority owner, and a newly expanded repurchase program plus a 2.4% dividend increase. (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Memory supply constraints” were highlighted as driving ASP increases and longer lead times, with management noting some projects “being indefinitely deferred” due to product unavailability. (Executive VP & CFO Michael Zilis)
  • “The volatile situation in the Middle East” was cited as a potential EPS headwind and as contributing to “shipping delays.” (Executive VP & CFO Zilis)
  • Management described mitigation through alternatives and bundling, saying Xvantage intelligence can recommend “substitute configuration, bundled product solutions, alternative vendor suppliers.” (CEO & Director Paul Bay)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a demand-and-execution quarter supported by Cloud and Advanced Solutions growth, alongside expanding Xvantage-driven automation (including email-to-order and IDA-led selling). For Q2, leadership guided to $13.6 billion to $14.0 billion in net sales and $0.68 to $0.78 in non-GAAP EPS, while calling out ongoing memory supply constraints and a potential $0.01 to $0.03 EPS impact tied to Middle East volatility, and pairing the outlook with increased capital returns via an expanded repurchase program and a higher quarterly dividend.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: XPeng hits 31,011 April deliveries, up 13% M/M; launches flagship GX SUV and expands European production
Idacorp reaffirms $6.25-$6.45 2026 EPS guidance while trimming hydropower outlook to 5.5M-7.0M MWh
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: IDACORP (IDA) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “IDACORP's diluted earnings per share were $1.21 compared with $1.10 last year,” and “we're reaffirming our full year 2026 IDACORP earnings guidance estimate in the range of $6.25 to $6.45 diluted earnings per share,” Amy Shaw (VP of Finance, Compliance & Risk) said.
  • “We've seen an overall customer increase of 2.3% since last year's first quarter,” and “industrial energy sales grew by 5.7%,” Lisa Grow (CEO, President & Director) said, adding, “we're starting to see the ramp-up in loads and revenues from some of our large industrial customers, and that ramp will accelerate during the year.”
  • “Legislation was passed in Idaho this year that effectively codified the way we currently develop large load contracts,” Grow said, noting “one change… established a deadline of 9 months for the PUC's contract approval process.”
  • “I'm happy to report that the Idaho Commission approved our 2026 Wildfire Mitigation Plan earlier this month,” Grow said.
  • “We've received a CPCN… for the company-owned 167-megawatt… natural gas plant” near Bennett Mountain, and the company “filed 4 CPCNs in Idaho for 2 additional natural gas plants,” Grow said, describing plans to bring “the 222-megawatt South Hills project online in 2029” and “the 430-megawatt Peregrine project in 2030.”
  • “We plan to make filings in the next couple of months with the Oregon and Idaho Commissions and FERC for the approval of the sale,” Grow said, referencing the previously announced Oregon service area divestiture.
  • “We saw strong results even with unusually mild weather and several expected headwinds,” Brian Buckham (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer) said.

Outlook

  • “Our key operating metrics and guidance are unchanged, except for our hydropower generation forecast as we reduced the top end of the range,” Shaw said.
  • “We expect IDACORP's diluted earnings per share this year to be in the range of $6.25 to $6.45,” John Wonderlich (Investor Relations Manager) said.
  • “We still expect that Idaho Power will use less than $30 million of additional investment tax credit amortization in 2026,” Wonderlich said.
  • “We continue to expect full year O&M expense to be in the range of $525 million to $535 million,” and “we still anticipate spending between $1.3 billion and $1.5 billion on CapEx in 2026,” Wonderlich said.
  • “We expect hydropower generation to be within the range of 5.5 million to 7.0 million megawatt hours for the year,” Wonderlich said, adding, “we trimmed the top end of our guidance.”

Financial results

  • “IDACORP's diluted earnings per share were $1.21,” Shaw said.
  • “IDACORP's first quarter net income increased over $8 million compared to last year,” Buckham said, attributing the increase to “higher retail revenues from the January rate increase and from customer growth combined for a $23 million benefit,” while “usage on a per customer basis decreased operating income by $10.7 million.”
  • “We saw increased FCA revenues of over $19 million compared to the first quarter of 2025,” Buckham said.
  • “O&M expenses were up $13.1 million compared to the first quarter of 2025,” and “depreciation and amortization expense increased around $6 million,” Buckham said.
  • “Nonoperating expense increased about $4 million, which was mostly higher interest expense,” Buckham said.
  • “Idaho Power amortized $6.3 million of additional tax credits… in the first quarter,” Buckham said, adding, “that was $13 million less than what we reported in the first quarter of 2025.”

Q&A

  • David Arcaro, Morgan Stanley: asked about rate case timing; CEO Grow answered, “we'll keep doing the math,” and CFO Buckham cited “the conversion of QIP plant in service” and “large load revenues” as factors.
  • Arcaro, Morgan Stanley: asked about large-load pipeline and ability to serve new load; CEO Grow said, “the pipeline is… well into the 2030s,” while COO Adam Richins said, “we feel good we're staying ahead of it.”
  • Whitney Mutalemwa, Wells Fargo: asked about credit positioning and more frequent rate relief; CFO Buckham said, “we've… target[ed] an average 50-50 debt equity capital ratio,” and CEO Grow said rate relief depends on whether “revenues… aren't matching up” with spend.
  • Christopher Ellinghaus, Siebert Williams Shank: asked about ITC recognition; CFO Buckham said, “we're actually a cash taxpayer,” and “no planned external monetization through sale of the tax credits.”
  • Ellinghaus, Siebert Williams Shank: asked about hotter/drier forecast and irrigation; CEO Grow said conditions “could be actually closer to normal,” and COO Richins said “low water years have not correlated to less sales.”
  • Michael Lonegan, Barclays: asked about Micron Fab 2 ESA and capital plan; COO Richins said, “we're still negotiating Fab 2 ESA,” and added Micron “anticipate initial wafer output… around mid-2027” for the first fab.
  • Lonegan, Barclays: asked about 2032 RFP ownership and CapEx; CEO Grow said, “historically, we've won about 50% of those,” and CFO Buckham said the CapEx forecast “doesn't have any resources for the 2032 RFP.”
  • Lonegan, Barclays: asked about equity issuance profile; CFO Buckham said, “anything incremental above that to maintain our balance sheet structure, assume 50-50,” and “definitely not linear.”
  • Brian Russo, Jefferies: asked about Micron Fab 2 in the load forecast and RFP process changes; COO Richins said, “Fab 2 is not in the 8.3%,” and the process is “faster than it was under the Oregon rules.”
  • Anthony Crowdell, Mizuho: asked if the “at least 200 megawatts” 2032 RFP ties to committed load; COO Richins said it is “tied to the 8.3% IRP growth rate.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly negative, centering on cadence of rate cases and credit metrics, including Mutalemwa’s question referencing “heavier CapEx cycle and just weaker near-term credit metrics.”
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A; Grow said, “it continues to amaze me how strong the pipeline is,” while Buckham emphasized “balance sheet strength is the most important thing for us.”
  • Versus last quarter, management maintained similar confidence on large-load growth and staying out of a near-term rate case, while adding more specificity on project approvals (e.g., CPCNs) and updating hydro expectations.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Full-year 2026 EPS guidance was unchanged: Q1 reaffirmed “$6.25 to $6.45,” consistent with Q4 when management “initiated” the same range.
  • Hydropower guidance narrowed on the high end: Q4 was “5.5 million to 7.5 million megawatt hours,” while Q1 was updated to “5.5 million to 7.0 million megawatt hours.”
  • Large-load contracting emphasis was consistent, while Q1 added that Idaho legislation “codified” contract development and imposed a “deadline of 9 months” for approvals.
  • Financing commentary shifted from Q4’s longer-term forecast discussion to Q1 execution detail, including Buckham’s note that the company “executed on $165 million of forward sales through our ATM program.”

Risks and concerns

  • “Unusually mild weather” reduced usage, Buckham said, and management reiterated guidance assumes “normal weather” and “normal power supply expenses.”
  • Buckham cited cost pressures from “higher depreciation and interest expense,” and Grow referenced “wildfire mitigation costs,” while pointing to large-load revenues as an offset.
  • On resource adequacy, Grow said new gas plants and storage are intended to address “our near-term capacity deficit,” and the company launched a 2032 RFP aimed at “a projected capacity deficit of at least 200 megawatts.”

Final takeaway

Management reiterated a steady 2026 earnings framework while highlighting accelerating large industrial load ramps (including Micron and Meta), continued execution on major transmission and dispatchable generation additions, and an active financing plan anchored on maintaining a 50-50 capital structure. The quarter’s notable update was a lower top-end hydropower outlook, while management framed new Idaho legislation, approved wildfire mitigation planning, and ongoing large-load contracting as key supports for growth, affordability, and system build-out through 2028 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: LPL forecasts 2026 core G&A at $2.155B-$2.19B as it plans $125M Q2 buybacks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: LPL Financial (LPLA) Q1 2026

Management view

  • CEO Richard Steinmeier said Q1 was “a strong start to the year” with “solid organic asset growth,” progress “to onboard Commonwealth Financial Network,” and “meaningful progress driving improved operating leverage,” while noting “rising macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty” and “an increasingly loud and often speculative narrative around the role of artificial intelligence and wealth management.”
  • Steinmeier reported that “total assets decreased to $2.3 trillion” and the firm “attracted organic net new assets of $21 billion,” and added that “recruited assets improved to $17 billion,” with the “overall pipeline” pushed “to record levels.”
  • On Commonwealth, Steinmeier said “asset retention… is in the mid-80s today” and “we continue to track towards our target of 90% retention,” while emphasizing preserving “Commonwealth’s indispensable approach to adviser satisfaction” and building “a comprehensive case management solution” to modernize service and operations.
  • CFO Matthew Audette said the quarter reflected “strong first quarter business and financial performance,” highlighting “adjusted pretax margin of approximately 38% and record adjusted EPS of $5.60,” and added that LPL “opportunistically resumed buybacks earlier this month with roughly $125 million planned for Q2.”

Outlook

  • Audette guided to 2026 core G&A of “$2.155 billion to $2.19 billion,” stating the company is “lowering the upper end of our outlook range by $20 million,” and guided Q2 core G&A to “$540 million to $560 million.”
  • Audette said, “we expect our payout rate will increase approximately 50 basis points in Q2,” and that “based on where client cash balances and interest rates are today, we expect our ICA yield to be roughly flat” in Q2.
  • Audette guided Q2 service and fee revenue to “increase by approximately $5 million,” Q2 transaction revenue to “decline by roughly $5 million,” and Q2 TA loan amortization to “increase by roughly $10 million.”
  • Compared with Q4’s buyback stance (“we paused buybacks… with a plan to revisit following the onboard”), Q1 included an explicit restart: Audette said LPL “resumed buybacks” with “roughly $125 million planned for Q2.”

Financial results

  • Adjusted EPS was $5.60 vs. analysts’ $5.47.
  • Steinmeier said “total assets decreased to $2.3 trillion” as “organic growth was more than offset by lower equity markets,” and Audette said total client assets were “down slightly from Q4.”
  • Audette reported gross profit of “$1.593 billion,” and said commission and advisory fees net of payout were “$487 million,” with a payout rate of “87.2%.”
  • Audette reported client cash revenue of “$460 million” and ending client cash balances of “$59 billion,” adding that within ICA, “the mix of fixed rate balances ended the quarter at roughly 60%,” and ICA yield was “336 basis points.”
  • On Commonwealth economics, Audette said LPL “now estimate[s] run rate EBITDA of approximately $410 million once fully integrated,” and attributed the change to “market-driven decline in Q1 assets.”

Q&A

  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe Research: asked about “structural headwinds to cash flow” from “Agentic AI tools” and “a more fee-based model”; CEO Steinmeier responded, “we don’t see an imminent risk,” but “we’re doing the work… to properly assess the opportunities and risks of reducing our reliance on cash sweep economics over time.”
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: asked about “incremental M&A” vs. repurchases; CFO Audette said near-term focus is “integrating Commonwealth” plus “clear and compelling returns on buying back our stock,” and Steinmeier added LPL will remain disciplined across “growing our markets,” “liquidity and succession,” and “capability transactions.”
  • Craig Siegenthaler, BofA Securities: asked why adviser count fell while retention was strong; Audette said it was “a near-term dynamic with respect to Commonwealth” as departures flow through headcount.
  • Devin Ryan, Citizens JMP: asked how AI affects productivity and consolidation; Steinmeier said LPL views AI as “an incredibly powerful tool… and not as a replacement,” outlining use cases in adviser tools, “straight-through processing,” and faster software development.
  • Y. Cho, JPMorgan: asked about recruiting momentum and April; Steinmeier said the pipeline is “at record levels,” while Audette said April cash reflected “advisory fees… around $2.5 billion” and taxes reducing cash “about $3 billion,” and that April organic growth would be “around 1.5%.”
  • Christopher Allen, KBW: asked about lower Commonwealth EBITDA run rate; Audette said the move from “$425 million to $410 million” was “all market-driven,” and if markets hold, “I would expect us to be back at $425 million.”
  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: asked if anything “under the surface” impacted EBITDA; Audette said, “No, not at all… No changes to expected synergies.”
  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: pressed on why AI won’t increase cash sorting and what “work” entails; Steinmeier said cash sorting “has already happened” and cash is “around $5,000 per account,” while Audette said LPL has “straightforward fee-based levers.”
  • Jeffrey Schmitt, William Blair: asked about Commonwealth synergy drivers; Audette cited custody/clearing revenue items “whether it be cash sweep” and “sponsor-related revenues,” plus “typical expense synergies.”
  • Michael Brown, UBS: asked if AI platforms threaten the adviser model; Steinmeier emphasized “trust and extended relationship” and said AI can “commoditize” low-value tasks while enhancing personalization.
  • Wilma Jackson Burdis, Raymond James: asked payout drivers; Audette said, “It was Commonwealth,” citing larger AUM advisers and “noise that’s unique to Q1.”
  • Y. Cho, JPMorgan (follow-up): asked where efficiency gains came from; Audette pointed to AI in service/operations and said certain processes could see “90%” cycle-time reduction.
  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe Research (follow-up): asked about long-term organic growth and enterprise pipeline; Steinmeier said LPL believes it can sustain “mid- to high single-digit growth rate over the long term,” and cited institutional momentum, including Prudential “thriving.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to skeptical, concentrating on cash monetization durability and AI disruption risk, including “structural headwinds to cash flow” (Chubak) and “AI disruption risk” (Brown).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and explanatory; Steinmeier said, “we don’t see an imminent risk,” and framed AI as “an extension and continuation of our strategy,” while Audette repeatedly emphasized “no changes to expected synergies” on Commonwealth.
  • Versus Q4’s focus on Commonwealth retention headlines, Q1’s questioning broadened to AI/cash economics and capital allocation, while management shifted from “plan to revisit” buybacks to having “resumed buybacks.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • In Q1, Steinmeier said assets “decreased to $2.3 trillion,” versus Q4’s “record $2.4 trillion.”
  • Q1 adjusted EPS was “$5.60,” versus Q4’s “$5.23.”
  • Q1 organic net new assets were “$21 billion,” versus Q4’s “$23 billion.”
  • Commonwealth EBITDA language changed from Q4 “approximately $425 million” to Q1 “approximately $410 million,” with Audette stating the shift was “all market-driven” and reversible if markets hold.
  • Capital return language tightened from Q4’s potential timing refinement to Q1’s explicit plan: “roughly $125 million planned for Q2.”

Risks and concerns

  • Steinmeier highlighted “macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty” and addressed AI uncertainty as an “often speculative narrative.”
  • Management acknowledged ongoing sensitivity to cash economics; Steinmeier said LPL is assessing “reducing our reliance on cash sweep economics over time,” while emphasizing it must be a “fair value exchange.”
  • Integration execution remains central; Audette said Commonwealth onboarding remains “on track to onboard in the fourth quarter,” with retention “in the mid-80s today” against a “target of 90% retention.”

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a strong start, anchored by $21B of organic net new assets, record adjusted EPS of $5.60, and improved operating leverage, while preparing to onboard Commonwealth in Q4 with mid-80% asset retention tracking toward a 90% target. Executives addressed investor focus on AI and cash monetization by emphasizing existing cash-sorting behavior and “fee-based levers,” and they signaled a notable capital allocation shift by resuming buybacks with roughly $125M planned for Q2 alongside a narrowed 2026 core G&A outlook range of $2.155B to $2.19B.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Rivian revises DOE loan down to $4.5B for Georgia factory
data.description:
Rivian (RIVN) has revised its loan agreement with the U.S. Department of Energy, now planning to draw up to $4.5B to fund construction of its Georgia factory, reduced from the $6.6B initially allocated during the Biden administration.

The company said Thursday it now plans to begin drawing on its loan in early 2027, earlier than previously expected, while increasing the Georgia plant’s initial production capacity to 300,000 vehicles annually from 200,000.

The 50% boost in first-phase capacity is aimed at lowering per-unit costs and strengthening operating leverage while also leaving room for further expansion in subsequent phases, the company said.

The company added that production of its upcoming R2 model is still slated to begin in late 2028 at the Georgia facility, following the recent start of production at its existing plant in Normal, Illinois.

CEO RJ Scaringe told CNBC’s Phil LeBeau that any future expansion of the Georgia plant would be funded internally, as Rivian continues to raise capital through partnerships with companies including Volkswagen and Uber.

The revised loan details were announced alongside Rivian’s Q1 results. Consolidated revenue was up 11.3% year over year to $1.38B, but loss from operations amounted to $881M compared to $655M a year ago, due to lower gross profit and higher operating expenses.

Rivian (RIVN) produced 10,236 vehicles at its manufacturing facility in Normal, Illinois, during the quarter and delivered 10,365 vehicles during the same period. By comparison, Rivian (RIVN) produced 14,611 vehicles and delivered 8,640 vehicles in Q1 of 2025.

data.title: Blue Owl sold half of SpaceX's stake at a $1.25T valuation
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital (OWL) co-CEO Marc Lipschultz said that the company has sold about half of its stake in SpaceX (SPACE) at a $1.25T valuation, crystallizing roughly a tenfold return.

"We made about 10x our money," and "we’ve sold about half of it at a $1.25 trillion valuation." Lipschultz said on an earnings call, still holding about half of it.

Blue Owl (OWL) was among SpaceX’s earliest lenders and later converted its exposure into an equity investment, Lipschultz said. "We made a loan to the company and had ⁠the ​privilege of getting to know them very well and ​then participating in ongoing conversations about other financing opportunities," he added.

The initial investment was made in 2021 through Blue Owl Technology Finance (OTF), with ~$27M deployed. The position had grown to about $195M in fair value by the end of 2025.

Separately, another Blue Owl-managed vehicle, Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), also held a smaller stake in SpaceX valued at about $21.7M at the end of 2025.

Lipschultz added that, in case of credit losses, sales of this kind "are the ways ... we ​can offset some of those losses."

data.title: MPS raises enterprise data growth floor to ~85% while targeting $6B capacity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “In Q1, MPS achieved record quarterly revenue of $804 million, 7% higher than the fourth quarter of 2025 and 26% higher than the first quarter of 2025,” said Tony Balow.
  • Balow highlighted demand drivers and strategic expansion: “Our communications end market grew 33% sequentially on the strength of our power solutions for optical modules and switches,” and “MPS continued to grow our capacity past our original $4 billion plan with a new goal of reaching $6 billion in the near future.”
  • On product expansion, Balow said, “We sampled our first high-speed interface products for DDR5 at major customers,” and positioned the longer-term shift: “MPS…accelerate our transition from chips only to a full-service silicon-based solution provider.”
  • CEO Michael R. Hsing emphasized competitive differentiation and execution: “We are the best in the market segment because we provide a total monolithic power solutions,” and “we don't want to be the dominant suppliers…our goal is to diversify growth.”

Outlook

  • Management raised its Enterprise Data growth framing vs. last quarter: “In the last call, we kind of rose that to a 50% floor…at this point in time, I think we're comfortable raising that floor up to around 85% year-over-year growth,” said Tony Balow.
  • On gross margin direction and caution, Balow said, “For Q2…we did have the confidence to increase incrementally our gross margins…[but] we do…see some strong headwinds potentially in the second half. And so we're not…remaining cautious for the guidance in the second half of the year.” (No specific Q2 revenue, EPS, or gross margin percentage guidance was stated in the transcript.)
  • On end-market pacing, Balow reiterated: “Auto…would be roughly flat for the first half of the year and ramping in later in the year,” and for Storage & Compute, “we're still very optimistic on storage…more cautious on notebook.”

Financial results

  • The company reported “record quarterly revenue of $804 million,” and tied the upside to mix and end-market strength, including “communications…33% sequentially.”
  • On profitability framework, Balow said, “For the last 4 quarters, we've been flat at 55.5%,” describing it as “at the low end of our gross margin model…55…to upper 50s,” while Hsing added, “I don't want to give you a false hope that we're going to jump very high.”
  • On pricing/cost actions to protect profitability, Hsing said, “some of the cost is higher…so we will keep…the goal is that we're keeping our margin profiles,” and Balow added, “in those cases…you could see us raise prices to stay within our gross margin model.”

Q&A

  • Ross Seymore, Deutsche Bank: Asked about “XPU…versus…CPU” demand and storage/computing tailwinds; Tony Balow: “CPU being a tailwind…we continue to see that here in 2026,” and on Storage & Compute, “very optimistic about storage staying strong…much more cautious on the notebook side.”
  • William Stein, Truist: Asked about capacity geography and “latest BCD generation”; CEO Michael R. Hsing: “We are still around 60 nanometers and maybe we'll go down to 40, 45 nanometers,” and Tony Balow: “very geographically diverse, both inside and outside of China.”
  • Joshua Buchalter, TD Cowen: Asked for segment guideposts and CPU vs AI upside split; CEO Hsing: “I'm not going to give it to you,” while Tony Balow: “we're comfortable raising that floor up to around 85% year-over-year growth…[and] we don't want to try to parse out between volume and content.”
  • Richard Schafer, Oppenheimer: Asked if supply limits upside and about robotics timing; CEO Hsing: “MPS is one of the players,” and on robotics, “We see this year…volume are still low…very difficult to predict,” while Balow said MPS is “trying to engage broadly and win all the designs we can.”
  • Quinn Bolton, Needham: Asked if comms could outgrow Enterprise Data and about 800V plus GaN/SiC; CEO Hsing: “we're not in the business to predicting…market trend,” and “we based on silicon carbide,” adding, “we developed our GaN…not for 800 volts…for lower voltage.”
  • Tore Svanberg, Stifel: Asked about 2,000W GPUs differentiation and DDR5 interface revenue timing; CEO Hsing cited “monolithics…integrations…modules” and “fully automated” test/reliability, while Tony Balow: “I wouldn't really have that as being a contributor to 2026 revenue.”
  • Gary Mobley, Loop: Asked comms content, channel inventory, and pricing; Tony Balow: “we're not going to talk about specific content,” “the channel has been very lean,” and on pricing, “there are places where input costs have gotten higher…[and]…raise prices.”
  • Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo: Asked why revenue acceleration doesn’t translate to big GM move and about robotics content; Tony Balow: “headwinds potentially in the second half,” and CEO Hsing: “for the dollar content…it's very difficult to say.”
  • Christopher Caso, Wolfe: Asked Merchant vs ASIC in Enterprise Data and auto visibility; CEO Hsing: “we don't know how to separate it,” and Tony Balow said auto ramp expectations are tied to “designs that we previously won coming to market.”
  • Wei Chia, Citi: Asked SiC vs GaN rationale and supply-chain readiness; CEO Hsing: “I still don't believe for high power,” and described inventory strategy: “we have actively preemptively…build these inventory,” while Tony Balow: “the 85% floor was [not] limited by something, that's not an issue right now.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with repeated emphasis on upside capture and sustainability, including Schafer’s “wow…on the outlook” and multiple questions probing constraints, content, and gross margin durability.
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks (record revenue, comms strength, $6B capacity goal), and more guarded/deflective in Q&A on content splits, with CEO Hsing stating, “I'm not going to give it to you,” and Balow repeatedly declining to “parse out between volume and content.”
  • Versus last quarter, the tone shifted toward higher confidence in Enterprise Data visibility (floor raised), while maintaining caution on second-half margin headwinds.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Enterprise Data framing increased from Q4’s “a floor of 50%” (Executive VP & CFO Bernie Blegen) to Q1’s “around 85% year-over-year growth” (Tony Balow).
  • Manufacturing capacity messaging advanced from Q4’s “more than $4 billion of geographically balanced capacity” (Balow) to Q1’s “new goal of reaching $6 billion in the near future” (Balow).
  • Leadership context changed from Q4’s CFO transition (“Bernie will be retiring…Rob Dean…interim CFO”) to Q1 execution under “Rob Dean, Interim CFO,” with prepared remarks led by Balow.
  • Analyst focus remained heavily concentrated on Enterprise Data/AI power, comms (optical modules/switches), and 800V architectures; Q1 added more explicit probing on pricing, gross margin cadence, and robotics monetization.

Risks and concerns

  • Geopolitics and macro uncertainty remained a stated overhang: “We continue to adjust to the fluid geopolitical and macroeconomic environment,” said Tony Balow.
  • Margin risk was concentrated in timing and cost pressures: Balow warned of “strong headwinds potentially in the second half,” and noted expedited supply-chain costs may require pricing actions.
  • Demand variability risk was explicit in notebooks and customer ramps: Balow said MPS is “more cautious on the notebook side,” while both Hsing and Balow repeated that MPS “can't control when the customers ramp.”

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 around record $804 million revenue, a sharp sequential jump in communications, and a larger manufacturing ambition (capacity goal raised to $6 billion). The most material update versus last quarter was the Enterprise Data outlook language, with management lifting its year-over-year growth “floor” to ~85% based on extended ordering patterns and backlog visibility, while simultaneously signaling second-half gross margin headwinds and continued reluctance to quantify content by platform or customer.
